title: practical english usage - Part 142
content:
Daniel; Daniel talks to Susan.) Susan and Daniel talk to each other on the phone every day. (Susan talks to Emily talks to Emily.) Jack and Emily are strange: they talk to themselves a lot. (Jack talks to Jack; Note the difference between -selves and each other / one another. Compare: 4 -selves,       possessives         and   each      other      I one     another They'll sit for hours looking into each other's / one another's eyes.

Both expressions have possessive forms.

3   each      other's       / one     another's carefully to what each other said.) notusually 7 hey each listened carefully to what the other said, (                They listened occasionally happens in subordinate clauses in very informal speech).

Each other and one another are not normally used as subjects (though this 2  not   used    as  subject grammar •  180   somebody, someone, anybody, anyone, etc For question tags after everything and nothing, ►    306.5.   For some time, sometime and sometimes, ►    590.

For the difference between no one and none, ►     164.

sometime eke.

or notime, and any time is written as two words. We   say some other time, not Sometime (►  590) is not exactly part of this group. There are no words everytime 8  sometime There aren't any cakes left - they've eaten every one.

Everyone had a good time at the party.

There is a similar difference between everyone and every one. Compare: You can borrow any one book at a time.

Does anyone know where Celia lives?

Anyone means the same as anybody; any one means 'any single one (person or 7 anyone        and   any     one;    everyone          and   every      one Let's go someplace quiet.

are common.

In informal American English, someplace, anyplace, everyplace and no place 6 someplace There's nothing much  on TV tonight.

We didn't do anything much  yesterday.

Note also the informal use of much  after any- and no-.

I don't like this place - let's go somewhere else.

Emily - are you in love with somebody else?

They  can also be followed by else (►  447).

I feel like eating something hot.  Let's go somewhere quiet this weekend.

She's going to meet someone in the Ministry.

I hope he marries somebody nice.

Somebody, etc can be followed by adjectives or adverbial expressions.

5 complementation:            somebody           nice,     etc Someone left their umbrella on the bus. Nobody phoned, did they'?

'There's somebody at the door.' 'Tell them I'm busy.' If anybody wants a ticket for the concert, they can get it from my office.

somebody, etc (►  175).

They, them and their are often used with a singular meaning to refer back to 4 use   of  they There are some people outside who want to talk to you.

There's somebody outside who wants to talk to you.

Somebody normally refers to only one person. Compare: Is everything ready? (xor Are everything ready?) Everybody likes her. (x<n EwrylnxIyMke-her;) When  these words are subjects they are used with singular verbs.

grammar •  181   one, you and they: used for people in general You/your/yourself can be used in similar ways.

One should always give oneself plenty of time to pack.

He talks to one like a teacher.  One's family can be very difficult.

pronoun oneself.

One can be a subject or object; there is a possessive one's and a reflexive 5 one/you        as  subject,     object,     etc in witehes - this could not include the speaker or hearer.) not In the sixteenth century people believed in witches, (     ... one/you believed One/You must believe in something.

for people who  could not include the hearer. Compare: One is not used for people who  could not include the speaker; you is not used 4 people     including       the   speaker/hearer not We speak a strange dialect where I come from, (     One speaks-a-strange whole groups.

One generally has a singular meaning: 'any individual'; it is not used to refer to not Thanks, I'm being served, (     Thanks-one-is-serving-me;) - It can take you/one ages to get served in this pub.

Somebody's knocking at the door, (     θne-is-teneeking-...) - One/You should knock before going into somebody's room.
